data.title: How Putwrite ETFs Seek Option-Premium Income: A Deep Dive With Overlay Shares
data.description:
By Eric McArdle

If you work with income-focused investors, you’ve probably heard some version of this question: “Is there any way to generate more income without taking on risks I don’t understand?”

It’s not an easy problem. Bond markets have gone through large interest rate shifts, equities can be volatile, and many investors still remember how “hidden risks” surfaced in past drawdowns.

This has led some advisors and institutions to explore options-based strategies, including putwrite ETFs, as one possible component of an income-oriented portfolio. While this blog discusses put-writing concepts broadly, Overlay Shares implements the strategy through put spreads, pairing each short put with a lower-strike long put to establish a defined-risk options overlay.

First: What is a putwrite ETF?

At a high level, a putwrite ETF is an exchange-traded fund that:

  1. Sells (“writes”) put options on a market index (often the S&P 500 Index), and

  2. Holds collateral - such as cash, short-term fixed income, or an underlying ETF - to support the obligations of those options.

When the ETF sells a put option, it:

  • Receives an upfront option premium, and

  • Takes on the obligation to absorb some downside if the index falls below the strike price of the option.

If the index finishes above the strike price at expiration, the put option may expire worthless, and the strategy keeps the premium. If the index falls below the strike price, the put seller may incur losses that can partially or fully offset, or exceed, that premium.

In simple terms, the strategy is agreeing to take on some downside risk in exchange for option premium income, within a rules-based framework.

Why option premiums may exist in the market

One factor that has influenced option pricing over time is the historical tendency for implied volatility to exceed the volatility the market ultimately realizes. This relationship - often referred to as the volatility risk premium - has been observed across multiple market periods, although it can vary meaningfully and may not persist in the future.

It does not eliminate risk, and it should not be viewed as a guarantee of income or positive returns. However, understanding this market dynamic can help explain why option sellers may at times receive premiums for taking on equity market risk.

Why sell puts instead of calls?

When advisors hear “options-based income,” many immediately think of covered-call strategies. If the goal is to harvest option premium, though, there are important differences between selling calls and selling puts. The historical data behind the chart below helps illustrate this.

We start by comparing two option selling indexes: the Cboe S&P 500 BuyWrite Index (BXM, black) and the Cboe S&P 500 PutWrite T-W Index (PWT, blue). BXM consists of a portfolio of S&P 500 Index securities with a monthly at-the-money (ATM) call option sold against the portfolio.

PWT owns U.S. Treasury Bills and sells a monthly ATM put option on the S&P 500 Index. To reiterate: PWT does not include S&P 500 portfolio exposure. Over this period, investors in a systematic treasury bill + putwrite strategy (PWT) have outperformed the covered-call overlay strategy (BXM) without having any direct equity beta exposure. The sample below shows data from 7/7/2014 – 12/31/2025.

Source: Ycharts | Results vary across market cycles and index performance is not indicative of any investable strategy. You cannot invest directly in the index

The pattern is intuitive. A covered-call strategy accepts call premium but gives up a portion of the market’s strongest upside moves, while still participating in most drawdowns. In long or powerful equity rallies, that forgone upside can more than offset the income.

By contrast, a putwrite strategy is not limited in its upside potential. Selling puts can allow investors to keep the full premium in flat or rising markets and use that premium to help absorb part of the damage in more moderate pullbacks.

Only when markets move meaningfully below the strike does the options sleeve begin to look and feel more like equity risk. Additionally, option premiums may not be sufficient to offset losses in declining markets.

For income-focused investors, the practical takeaway is that not all option-selling frameworks are created equal. For some investors, selling puts may offer a differentiated way to seek option-premium income across a full market cycle.

This is one of the reasons the Overlay Shares lineup is built around a disciplined, out-of-the-money put-writing framework, while still acknowledging that each approach will have periods where it is more or less favorable.

But not all putwrite strategies look alike. Two design choices can significantly affect how they behave:

  1. What collateral backs the options?

    Two broad approaches are common:

  • Cash-secured putwriting

    The fund sells put options and holds enough cash and/or short-term instruments to cover its obligations if the market declines sharply. This can help manage counterparty obligations, but it does not eliminate the risk of loss. If the market falls, the value of the options position can decline substantially.

  • Overlay on an existing portfolio

    Some strategies, including Overlay Shares, hold a core ETF exposure - such as a large-cap equity ETF or a core bond ETF - and then apply a put option overlay on top. In that case, investors are exposed both to:

    • The underlying asset class (equities or bonds), and

    • The performance of the options overlay.

The choice of collateral affects the strategy’s return pattern, risk exposures, and potential role in a portfolio.

  1. Single puts vs. put spreads

A second key choice is whether the strategy uses:

  • Single (“naked”) short puts

    The strategy sells a put option, collects premium, and remains exposed to additional losses as the index declines, potentially down to zero in an extreme scenario.

  • Put spreads

    A put spread is created by:

    • Selling a put at one strike price, and

    • Buying another put at a lower strike price.

The purchased (long) put is designed to create a floor on losses from the options position over each option cycle. While this does not protect the overall portfolio from losses in the underlying assets, it helps define the maximum loss attributable to the options sleeve.

Overlay Shares uses put spreads, rather than single short puts, within its overlay design.

How Overlay Shares uses a put spread overlay

Liquid Strategies manages the Overlay Shares family of ETFs, which focuses on combining core equity and fixed income ETF exposures with a put spread overlay using options on the S&P 500 Index.

Conceptually, the process looks like this:

  1. Core ETF exposure

    Each fund starts with a core allocation - such as:

    • An S&P 500 ETF (U.S. large-cap equities)

    • A small-cap equity ETF

    • A foreign equity ETF

    • A core bond ETF

    • A short-term bond ETF

    • A municipal bond ETF

  2. Put spread overlay on each ETF’s respective underlying index

    On top of that core, the strategy systematically:

      • Sells laddered, out-of-the-money (OTM) index put options, and

      • Purchases lower-strike index put options, forming put spreads

  3. Defined options risk framework

    Because each short put is paired with a lower-strike long put, the options sleeve has a defined maximum loss over each option cycle. However, the underlying holding still carries downside market risk. This does not eliminate equity or bond market risk, but it provides a structural framework for managing tail risk within the overlay itself.

  4. Application across multiple asset classes

The Overlay Shares ETF lineup applies this same overlay concept across:

    • U.S. large-cap equities

    • U.S. small-cap equities

    • Foreign equities

    • Core bonds

    • Short-term bonds

    • Municipal bonds

For some investors, the appeal is the combination of familiar core exposures with a systematic options overlay that is designed with risk controls on the derivatives sleeve.

Where might putwrite overlays fit in a portfolio?

Any decision to use a putwrite or overlay approach should be grounded in an investor’s objectives, risk tolerance, constraints, and time horizon. Conceptually, some investors and advisors evaluate strategies like Overlay Shares in a few broad contexts:

  1. Adjusting core equity allocations

    An investor who already holds a broad equity ETF might, together with their adviser:

  • Maintain a core allocation to a traditional equity ETF, and

  • Allocate a portion to an ETF that combines equity or bond exposure with a put spread overlay.

The idea is to stay connected to major markets while introducing a rules-based options component that may change the pattern of returns and distributions.

  1. Building a diversified “income sleeve”

    Some income-focused portfolios blend multiple sources of potential cash flow, such as:

  • Traditional fixed income

  • Dividend-oriented equities

  • Options-based strategies, including putwrite and overlay approaches

Because Overlay Shares applies a consistent overlay concept across multiple underlying asset classes (equities and fixed income), some investors view the family as one potential tool to diversify how income is generated—across coupons, dividends, and option premiums—recognizing that this does not eliminate risk but offers a potentially diversifying risk premium.

  1. Incorporating a defined options risk framework

    Investors who are comfortable with derivatives but prefer clearly defined parameters may gravitate toward structures, like put spreads, that establish a maximum loss for the options sleeve over each option cycle. This does not cap losses in the overall fund, but it offers a more transparent framework for understanding the risk contribution of the overlay itself.

In all of these cases, position sizing, monitoring, and rebalancing are critical and typically require ongoing review.

Risk Factors

Options strategies involve risk, including the potential for significant losses. Putwrite and put spread strategies may lose more than the premiums received, particularly in sharply declining markets. Premium income is not guaranteed and may vary with market conditions. Past performance of indices or strategies is not indicative of future results.

Selling (writing) and buying options are speculative activities and entail greater than ordinary investment risks. The Fund’s use of put options can lead to losses because of adverse movements in the price or value of the underlying asset, which may be magnified by certain features of the options. When selling a put option, the Fund will receive a premium; however, this premium may not be enough to offset a loss incurred by the Fund if the price of the underlying asset is below the strike price by an amount equal to or greater than the premium. Purchased put options may expire worthless and the Fund would lose the premium it paid for the option. The Fund may lose significantly more than the premiums it receives in highly volatile market conditions.

The Fund will invest in short term put options which are financial derivatives that give buyers the right, but not the obligation, to sell (put) an underlying asset at an agreed-upon price and date. The Fund’s use of options may reduce the Fund’s ability to profit from increases in the value of the underlying asset. The Fund could experience a loss or increased volatility if its derivatives do not perform as anticipated or are not correlated with the performance of their underlying asset or if the Fund is unable to purchase or liquidate a position.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CHW: Strong Dividend Coverage And Attractive Valuation
data.description:
Overview

With market indices trading near all-time highs, it can be challenging to find attractively valued opportunities in the market. Despite global funds seeing a rise over the last year, the Calamos Global Dynamic Income Fund (CHW) continues to trade at an attractive discount to NAV valuation. When I previously covered CHW, I issued a buy rating due to the potential to benefit from lower interest rates. Since my last coverage, the fund has released an updated annual report for 2025, so I wanted to reassess the fund's internal performance and current value proposition.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CHW's share price has increased by about 14.8%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to participate in the positive market momentum over the last year. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up above 23% over the same time frame. CHW now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 7.7%, while issuing payouts on a monthly basis. Based on the latest reporting, CHW has demonstrated its ability to generate earnings that far exceed distributions being paid out to shareholders.

The combination of the attractive price to NAV discount and strong dividend coverage makes CHW a great option for long-term buy-and-hold investors. CHW takes a blended approach to its portfolio strategy by providing direct equity exposure and a mix of income producing securities that allow the fund to remain consistent during shifting market cycles. This portfolio strategy is efficient since it can support the distributions with ease, but there is still room for improvement. For instance, the internal net investment income has consistently reported losses year over year, which is a sign of thin operating spreads. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that CHW implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, CHW has total managed assets of $760M that are spread across a blended portfolio of assets. The fund has an inception date going back to 2007, which means CHW is now approaching two decades of operating history. CHW has a listed expense ratio of 2.29% of assets, but this is a bit misleading because it includes expenses and fees related to interest and other operating costs. When excluding these operating costs, CHW's actual management fee sits at 1% of total assets.

The fund has the primary goal of providing attractive total returns while focusing on a high level of current income. In order to do this, the fund invests at least 40% of its assets in foreign securities, including things like emerging markets and other convertible funds that put an emphasis on income generation. However, management can dynamically shift its asset allocation based on market conditions. The portfolio's breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Common Stock: 61.1%
  • Convertibles: 20.4%
  • Corporate Bonds: 8.7%
  • Cash and Receivables/Payables" 4.3%
  • Other: 2.2%
  • Bank Loans: 1.6%
  • Sovereign Bonds: 1.0%
  • Options: 0.7%

Expanding on this diversity, CHW also maintains an efficient allocation across different sectors. As we can see below, CHW is most heavily allocated to information technology companies at 21.7% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to industrials and financials, which accounts for 14.1% and 13.5% of assets, respectively. Although the fund doesn't try to mimic any specific index, this focus on technology companies means that CHW can potentially capture some of the positive growth momentum led by the optimism around AI.

When looking at the top holdings of the fund, we can see many notable names in the top ten positions. For instance, the largest position is currently Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) at 4.3% of total assets. Additionally, we can see many other notable names as part of the fund's top holdings, including Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), The Boeing Company (BA), and Alibaba Group (BABA), just to name a few. However, we must remember some of these positions are positioned as income holdings, rather than common stock, so CHW may not be able to fully capture the price growth of some positions.

Valuation & Performance Insights

When I previously covered CHW about 6 months ago, the fund traded at a discount to NAV of 11.4%. Following the share price increase since then, CHW now trades at a smaller discount to NAV of 9.81%. For reference, CHW has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.47% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that CHW's price to NAV discount severely widened following 2022, which is when the Fed aggressively hiked interest rates to the highest range over the last ten-year period. So compared to the fund's historical range, CHW remains attractively valued.

Since the time of my last coverage, CHW has also released an updated annual report for 2025. This is the most updated report that reveals the details around the fund's earnings. Net investment income amounted to a loss of $0.05 per share, while net realized gains landed at $1.51 per share. Looking back on prior years, the fund has consistently struggled to generate positive net investment income, which can possibly be an indication of a flawed portfolio structure or the result of higher operating costs. Since net investment income is so weak, this leaves the fund entirely reliant on positive net realized gains to support NAV growth and provide distribution coverage.

The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on underlying market conditions. This is why the fund has experienced such large swings in its earnings, such as in 2022 when CHW experienced a large net realized loss of $3.15 per share. Despite market indices heading to new all-time highs in 2025, the earnings were still a bit lower than 2024's earnings. This makes it very difficult to estimate how consistent the fund's NAV growth will be. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $10.14 per share
  • 2022: $6.12 per share
  • 2023: $5.99 per share
  • 2024: $7.72 per share
  • 2025: $8.58 per share

Since the fund is so reliant on net realized gains, this essentially means that CHW is required to actively sell off the upside growth of its strongest positions. Therefore, some of the fund's positions aren't capable of simply increasing over time without interruption. This translates to minimal price growth for the fund over a longer holding period. For instance, CHW's share price has increased by 29.4% over the last decade, while the total return, including all distributions, jumps up to 243.9% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that 90% of their total return is likely to come from the fund's ability to sustain a high rate of payouts.

Being that CHW is a global fund, the short-term volatility related to potential Greenland conflict can cause the fund to experience some downside momentum. We have no idea how sentiment can shift, and since CHW is most heavily allocated to technology companies, the fund may not be able to remain resilient in a shift of capital out of technology, since technology is usually the first to see declines during periods of uncertainty.

Risk Profile

Despite the fund providing direct equity exposure to some of the highest quality companies in the world, the focus on income securities introduces a sensitivity to interest rates. Despite the Fed initiating three different 25 basis point cuts to interest rates, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. As a result, we can see that CHW's share price has struggled to get back to its pre-pandemic levels, which may be attributed to two main things:

  1. The Use Of Leverage
  2. Exposure To Securities With Low Credit Ratings

As part of the portfolio strategy, CHW utilizes leverage so that it can amplify returns when market conditions allow. The use of leverage currently amounts to 31.65% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$240M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage in this higher interest rate environment, considering the fact that leverage can also amplify losses if allocated to securities that do not perform as anticipated.

Since interest rates remain high, the underlying operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities may be thin. This can be a reason why the fund's net investment income continues to report losses year after year. I believe that lower interest rates can possibly serve as a catalyst for growth, but the challenge is that we don't know how long it will take for the interest rates to get back down to their pre-pandemic levels.

Another risk comes from the fund's exposure to lower rated securities, which can introduce a vulnerability to defaults. For reference, securities with a credit rating below a BBB are classified as below investment grade and may be higher risk due to their less consistent cash flows or their reliance on a debt to support operations. S&P Global estimates that the rate of speculative grade defaults can increase as high as 5.1% in a pessimistic outlook, assuming interest rates remain elevated. The issue is that the fund has a large percentage of its securities marked as unrated, so we have little transparency into how much of the portfolio may be considered as higher risk. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 21.2%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 30.2%
  • Unrated: 48.5%

Strong Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.05 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.7%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.60 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, we can see that the fund has no issues generating earnings that outpace the distributions being paid. For reference, here are the annual earnings over the last few years:

  • 2021: $2.95 per share
  • 2022: ($3.22) per share
  • 2023: $0.47 per share
  • 2024: $2.33 per share
  • 2025: $1.46 per share

As we can see, the only outlier over the last five years was the period where markets were shifting in response to interest rate fluctuations. In fact, CHW has demonstrated that its portfolio can efficiently generate earnings that can support several years worth of distributions in a single period. Therefore, CHW remains a solid choice for investors seeking a sense of stability and reliability through different market cycles. The fund's strength in earnings and dividend sustainability was demonstrated in this most recent 2025 period:

2025's earnings of $1.46 per share / $0.60 annual payout = 2.43 years.

However, the fund may not offer any tax efficiency for investors that want to use the distributions paid out by CHW. For instance, the latest Section 19(a) notice indicates that all of the distributions paid on a YTD basis were supported by short-term capital gains, which are taxed at the highest rates for investors. So if you are an investor that wants to minimize the tax liability of the distributions received within their portfolio, a position in CHW would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on CHW at this time due to the attractive price-to-NAV valuation and the fund's strong dividend coverage. After reviewing the latest annual reporting, the fund's earnings continue to demonstrate its ability to generate earnings that far exceed the distributions being paid. However, the fund is heavily dependent on positive net realized gains to be the driver of its earnings and NAV growth, so CHW may not produce strong results during market downturns. However, the efficient portfolio strategy makes this a great long-term buy-and-hold fund for investors that want to put an emphasis on reliable income generation on a monthly basis.

data.title: How YNVD:CA Monetizes Nvidia Without Sacrificing Upside
data.description:
My base case for Nvidia (NVDA) over the next 6-12 months is a range-bound to moderately upward biased path rather than another sharp rerating phase. After a multi-month time correction and several sentiment headwinds now reflected in valuation, earnings delivery and backlog visibility should limit downside while sustaining elevated option premiums. This regime favors income strategies that monetize volatility without requiring strong directional upside.

That outlook helps a covered call strategy like the NVIDIA (NVDA) Yield Shares Purpose ETF (YNVD:CA). This ETF has structural differences with another Nvidia-based option strategy I have covered in the past - NVDY - but retains the core option writing strategy. Hence, I will discuss YNVD:CA in this thesis, while referencing NVDY as an already discussed and more familiar Nvidia covered call strategy. That helps understand YNVD:CA's structure with a reference context and anchor expectations ahead. In other words, NVDY is very simplistically speaking, an unleveraged version of YNVD:CA. So, the reference to NVDY helps understand what changes in YNVD:CA's thesis due to the introduction of leverage.

NVDY is also a Buy in my view. The Buy call on NVDY (as discussed at length in my NVDY thesis) is not completely reliant on Nvidia's bull thesis, but the positive view on Nvidia also helps - NVDY can work with a neutral to mildly bullish NVDA outlook. I find the idea of option writing as a means to enhance income particularly suited to Nvidia's volatility profile - high enough to generate meaningful option premiums, but not so extreme that premium capture breaks down. NVDY also provides better downside cushioning compared to holding Nvidia. It performs well when NVDA is range-bound, slowly correcting, or grinding higher, like the regime seen in the past 1-2 months, allowing the option layer to actually add value.

Some of the reasons why NVDY works very well are applicable to YNVD:CA too. Like the underlying volatility levels and an overall bullish long term thesis in Nvidia. The drawdown cushioning in what is conceptually the Canadian sibling of NVDY is not as strong, but the upside capture is stronger. Evidence of alpha in flat markets has also shown up recently. YNVD:CA uses active leverage and partial option coverage techniques to maximize total returns, supporting a less NAV-eroding payout than we see in NVDY.

Overall, YNVD:CA is a Buy for income investors. The yields are more sustainable without pressuring the NAV. The balance of leverage and option writing leads to more dependence on a bullish thesis than an unleveraged variant like NVDY, but it also adds premium income when the underlying consolidates and captures better upsides - scenarios that are in line with my Nvidia thesis.

How It Does What It Does

YNVD:CA writes call options close to the money for up to 50% of the portfolio. At the time of writing, YNVD:CA had ~60% of the calls written at the money and ~35% in the money. That makes the option layer quite aggressive - works well to extract heavier premiums that drag upside capture in rallies as much as generate income otherwise.

What balances the aggressive close to the money positioning is its partial option coverage (~50% of the portfolio's notional value) that is actively managed and can be strengthened in times of flat or pressured expectations. Even if Nvidia rallies when the strategy is fully loaded in call writing (full 50% coverage implemented), the partial option writing cap ensures upsides can still be captured reasonably.

YNVD:CA also adds leverage of up to 25% on the underlying long Nvidia position. This is also an active layer, and the balance between option writing and leverage are expected to be tweaked in various regimes of Nvidia's share prices. The balance between how much call writing to do, where, and how much leverage to deploy on the underlying ends up shaping the total returns for the ETF. However, it is also true that the pursuit of total returns makes YNVD:CA more reliant on a bull thesis in Nvidia (compared to, say, NVDY, where upsides are compromised in lieu of better drawdown management or higher potential income in flat markets).

Net Result - Close Tracking With Alpha

In all my covered call ETF analyses, I keep emphasizing two kinds of ETFs that make for a Buy case. The first case is if the strategy helps dampen volatility - particularly by reducing drawdown levels. NVDY belongs to this category. The second kind of covered call strategy captures upsides better, and ends up tracking the underlying very well - a total return play in the long run that eventually funds the payouts better. YNVD:CA belongs to this category. The trade-off in these strategies is that they have higher drawdowns than NVDY-like implementations. That trade-off is not strictly necessary in theory because an active strategy can go lighter on leverage and heavier on option coverage in flat to bearish markets - and vice versa in bullish ones. That's easier said than done and requires active management to get it right consistently - an ideal state ask.

In other words, NVDY's style of play is better volatility management, while YNVD:CA plays for better total returns. Strictly going by numbers, NVDY saves ~4 percentage points in a sharp drop scenario (where Nvidia is down by ~30%). YNVD:CA actually goes down more than Nvidia in these conditions. But for long term returns, the upside capture that NVDY foregoes is more material - as we see in the total return charts from 2024. On the other hand, its drawdown mitigation is limited to premiums received. In the overall scheme of things, if Nvidia rallies strongly, the outperformance delivered by YNVD:CA is far larger than that delivered by NVDY in falling markets.

The total returns actually show YNVD:CA delivering more total returns than Nvidia over the period from 2024. This is expected because of its leverage amplifying returns. However, the same leverage does not mean commensurate drawdown magnification as seen in April 2025. Which means the balance between call writing and leverage works out very well.

While YNVD:CA needs a bullish thesis on the underlying more than an unleveraged covered call play, its does still generate income in flat market conditions. The alpha expected in flat markets are also showing up now, as Nvidia is consolidating. In the past couple of months, as Nvidia is only up by ~1.5%, both NVDY and YNVD:CA have capitalized on the option premiums - this gives a route to positive total returns even in a scenario where Nvidia's share prices remain pressured.

Yields and Sustainability

The lure of higher yields erodes NAV, which is not a necessary evil, but unless dividends are reinvested, investors often miss out on later recoveries if the initial payouts have been made through a period of bearish underlying moves. That makes dividends sustainable in yield terms, but absolute payouts diminish over time. Optically, the share price also shrinks to levels that call for reverse splits for optics (like we have seen in some YieldMax products already). Reinvestments in aggressive yield cases also mean tax implications. I like YNVD:CA's payout levels. It looks less NAV eroding, preserving capital for later bull rallies too. For context, refer to YieldMax's high yield style (NVDY's yields of ~75%, ignoring the mechanical expansion around April 2025 due to lower share prices, are far higher than the more reasonable ~25% levels YNVD:CA delivers).

A Good Income Buy

Overall, YNVD:CA's Buy call is backed by several supportive factors that are visible structurally. It retains the advantages of Nvidia as the optimally volatile and long-term bullish underlying. It aims for high upside capture in lieu of higher drawdowns (almost matching and potentially exceeding Nvidia's drawdowns marginally). That ensures better funding for the payouts over a full cycle. The yields also appear less aggressive and leave more chances for the share prices to even flourish along with the strong payouts (below chart showing NAV erosion effects in NVDY for context).

YNVD:CA is meant for higher total returns while monetizing consolidations too. That is exactly the regime I expect in Nvidia - flat to bullish. In the near term, Nvidia is likely to be earnings-led rather than multiple-driven, balancing intact demand and scale and valuation sensitivities. Its moat-like position is what should keep the near term bias bullish within a consolidation band. This is already evident in recent consolidation behavior of the past few months - something that should continue.

YNVD:CA is actually better equipped to handle positive surprises in case I am wrong and Nvidia breaks out again from this consolidation phase. The only scenario that poses a risk to my thesis is a heavy correction in Nvidia, which could happen due to broad market sell-offs or material earnings shocks (maybe an AI capex drop led). I view the probability of a deep correction as relatively low, as Nvidia’s near-term earnings are anchored by a visible backlog. In broader market sell-offs, I expect Nvidia to be relatively well supported as flight to quality dominates.

For long term investors, YNVD:CA's leverage should work out well in the long term, even if short term drawdowns emerge. The incremental downside risk that comes with the leverage and pursuit of total returns is not as high as the opportunity to be captured when Nvidia rallies again (as we saw in April 2025). Till it rallies, even consolidations should be well monetized.

data.title: Whale's Insight: Onchain Utility, Offchain Control
data.description:
NYSE Plans 24/7 Trading Platform for Tokenized Stocks and ETFs

The New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) announced plans to develop a blockchain-based trading platform later this year which would enable 24/7 trading of tokenized stocks and exchange-traded funds.

The platform will use multiple blockchain networks to enable real-time settlement, dollar-denominated order sizing, and stablecoin-based funding. Tokenized securities traded on the venue will be fungible with traditional issued shares with all shareholder rights, including dividends and governance participation.

Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), which is the parent company of NYSE, is developing a broader digital strategy leverage blockchain, which also includes working with major banks such as BNY and Citi to support tokenized deposits, enabling clearing members to move funds, meet margin requirements, and manage liquidity outside traditional banking hours.

Key Take

  • With tokenized real-world assets becoming a major narrative in the industry, this shift brings challenges for existing crypto companies. As seen with DTCC and now the NYSE stepping in, tokenization is increasingly being led by regulated financial incumbents. It is difficult for crypto-native firms to compete in these highly regulated areas, where established giants benefit from scale, regulatory status, and deep institutional trust.

  • This development also poses challenges for Layer 1 blockchains such as Ethereum (ETH-USD) and Solana (SOL-USD). Despite language about “supporting multiple blockchains” in the announcement, it’s far more likely that settlement will run on permissioned, institution-controlled networks, not open public chains. NYSE operates in one of the most heavily regulated environments. Settlement systems must support identity, access controls, reversibility, compliance, and supervisory oversight, requirements that are far easier to enforce on private or consortium blockchains than on permissionless networks.

  • In regulated tokenized asset markets, where investors already trust centralized institutions for issuance and custody, and comply with all regulatory requirements, the value proposition of decentralized and trustless public blockchains becomes less compelling.

Gold’s Rally Fuels Surge in Tokenized Gold Trading

Gold prices have been surging to all-time highs one after another, recording their strongest performance in nearly half a century. As geopolitical tensions intensified and tariff war threats resurfaced, both institutional and retail investors turned to gold as a defensive asset in a turbulent world.

Tokenized gold has been one standout beneficiary in this backdrop. Two top tokenized gold, Tether's XAUT (XAUT-USD) and PAX Gold (PAXG-USD) recorded explosive growth, with trading activity even surpassing most traditional gold exchange-traded funds.

While the total market cap of $4.4 billion for tokenized gold is still small compared to the $32 trillion global gold market, its rapid expansion highlights the appeal of the tokenized version of assets. Fractional ownership, low barriers to entry, and global accessibility make it particularly attractive to retail investors, especially in emerging markets, where most people lack other channels to invest in gold-related financial instruments.

Key Take

  • Beyond simple buying and holding, tokenized gold has opened up new use cases in DeFi. Crypto investors are deploying gold-backed tokens in various yield-generating strategies, putting their gold to work, while historically gold investing earning nothing in yield.

  • For example, Uniswap’s PAXG/USDC pool allows traders to swap between tokenized gold and the USD stablecoins. Liquidity providers in this pool earn trading fees. LPs earn passive income while maintaining exposure to gold, making it an appealing option, especially during periods of heightened gold interest and trading activity.

  • As the world has entered a commodity bull cycle, both precious and industrial metals have recorded strong performance over the past year. This creates a significant opportunity for DeFi, as traditional commodity investments do not generate yield, and the ability to earn yield represents a meaningful enhancement that could accelerate DeFi adoption.

Ethereum Activity Surge Masks Address-Poisoning Scams

Ethereum has recently recorded a sharp increase in daily transactions and active addresses, but the surge may not reflect genuine network growth according to studies both from Citi and crypto researchers. The spike in network activities is closely linked to address-poisoning scams rather than user adoption, and a large share of recent transactions is less than $1 value.

Onchain research shows that stablecoins account for roughly 80% of recent activity growth, driven by smart contracts sending tiny amounts of USDT and USDC to hundreds of thousands of wallets.

Since Ethereum has greatly reduced the transaction gas cost in the past year, it is now inexpensive for malicious actors to flood the network with small amount transfers which aimed to mimic legitimate wallet addresses to mislead users. When the victim copies an address from their history without fully verifying it, they might accidentally send funds to the scammer's wallet instead.

Key Take

  • Rising on-chain metrics do not necessarily equate to real network growth. Large-scale scam tactics such as address poisoning can significantly inflate transaction counts and active addresses. It is thus difficult to assess genuine adoption without deeper analysis.

  • While address-poisoning scams rarely succeed against experienced users, they pose a real risk for those who are not sufficiently careful. The tactic relies on routine behavior by using similar addresses that resemble those users commonly interact with, increasing the risk of accidental sending to the wrong addresses.

  • This also highlights a broader usability challenge for blockchains. The cost of malicious behavior is so low, while the burden of safety falls heavily on users themselves. Expecting everyone to exercise constant vigilance is not a viable path to mass adoption.

Weekly Market Chart: Polymarket’s Brand Overtakes the Category

Google search interest for “Polymarket” (POLYMARKET) has reached 100 in early 2026, its highest level on record, even surpassing the November 2024 U.S. election peak of 99, when the platform processed $3.7 billion in election-related volume. Notably, this new high comes without a comparable headline event, meaning Polymarket has moved beyond its image as an election-only platform to a general prediction market.

At the same time, search interest for the generic term “prediction market” has fallen to below 20 in January, indicating declining attention at the category level. An interesting comparison is Google, which became the default search engine as users began to simply “Google it.”

This pattern, rising brand recognition alongside falling category-level searches, suggests Polymarket may be on a similar path toward becoming the default destination for prediction markets, much as Google became synonymous with web search.

Source: Google

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: Post-Earnings Weakness Is An Opportunity
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (JPM) is the most valuable bank in the world, with a market cap of more than $850 billion. We discussed the bank in our last article as potentially the first trillion-dollar bank; however, it's slightly underperformed the S&P 500 since then, with risks around the company's relationship with the Trump admin and rising expenses. Despite that, the company's strong earnings show its strong potential and ability to generate continued rewards.

JPMorgan Chase 4Q Results

JPMorgan Chase had a strong 4Q, while continuing to maintain an incredibly strong portfolio.

The company had $13 billion in net income for the quarter, putting it at a P/E of just under 16x. The company had an overhead ratio of 51% with $24 billion of expenses and just under $47 billion worth of revenue. The company continued its modest dividend of just under 2%, with share repurchases at roughly 4%, putting it at 6% annualized returns.

JPMorgan Chase had $1.5 trillion in loans, up 9% YoY, with deposits of $2.6 trillion. With a strong capital ratio, JPMorgan Chase has continued to grow aggressively.

Looking at the breakdown of the company's FY25, we do see a slight net income decrease YOY. That's despite sizable revenue growth as the company's credit costs went up substantially. That was through both increased reserve building along with a slight increase in net charge-offs. However, the aggressive share repurchases helped the company to increase EPS slightly.

JPMorgan Chase continued to maintain a double-digit ROE, and the top-line revenue growth was still strong in an uncertain market. The company's expanding card services necessitated the reserve build, but we don't see that increase as a major long-term expense.

JPMorgan Chase Balance Sheet

At the same time, versus large banks, JPMorgan Chase continues to maintain a low-risk fortress balance sheet.

JPMorgan Chase has a massive $288 billion in CET1 capital with an advanced 14.1% CET1 capital ratio. Chase is taking over the Apple Card (AAPL), which includes a $20 billion loan portfolio expected to cause a 90 bps impact on the CET1 ratio. It's worth noting that as the company's ratio has slightly declined from distributions, this is a hit that could slow down capital distributions some.

JPMorgan Chase Forecast

JPMorgan Chase's forecast emphasizes why we expect the company to continue its growth towards a $1 trillion valuation.

The company's outlook for 2026 is ~3% growth in net interest income. Average loans firm-wide grew 10% YoY to 4Q 2025, and the company's card services have also grown substantially. Deposits have also grown at the mid-single digits firm-wide. This strong historic growth and guidance for low single-digit growth should provide continued returns.

This growth at the same P/E should take the company past a market cap of more than $900 billion, positioning it well for a $1+ trillion market cap.

It's worth noting that the company does expect adjusted expenses to grow substantially, and it has yet to provide guidance on its other non-interest income. The company is investing heavily in bankers, advisors, and branches, which will help it continue to position itself as a premium bank with the best offerings to customers.

That forecast helps make JPMorgan Chase a valuable investment.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is JPMorgan Chase's size and scale. There are not exactly customers in the U.S. who haven't heard about the company, and the company has spent years and years improving its operations. That means that while we expect the company to continue 6% returns, we don't see sizable growth ahead.

The other major risk for the company is its relationship with the Trump administration. The Trump admin has proposed a 10% cap on credit card interest rates, which JPMorgan Chase has called an economic disaster. At the same time, the company is facing a $5 billion lawsuit for "de-banking Trump". What happens remains to be seen, but these risks with a powerful president are worth noting.

Conclusion

JPMorgan Chase is the largest publicly traded bank in the world, with a $860 billion market cap. The company trades at a P/E of ~15x with a high single-digit earnings yield. The company is continuing to pay an almost 2% dividend yield, and it's aggressively repurchasing shares, both of which will enable the company to drive additional shareholder returns.

The company has continued to show itself as a dominant force. For example, the company is buying Apple Card, which will add tens of billions dollars of credit card assets. At the same time, the company will continue growing in future years with net interest income and non-interest income. That combination makes JPMorgan Chase a valuable investment.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: European Utilities To See Steady Currents In 2026
data.description:
By Nadège Tillier, Head of Corporate Sector Strategy

Call 1: The sector's cash flow generation stabilises

With the top 40 European utilities expected to generate around €186bn of EBITDA in 2026, the sector is projected to achieve 1% year‑on‑year growth. This growth will mostly be driven by pure network utilities, which should see their EBITDA grow by a robust 6%. Gas and electricity grid utilities should continue to benefit from a growing regulated asset base fuelled by ongoing investments to modernise, transform and expand the networks.

Modest cash flow generation growth in 2026

Top 40 European utilities aggregated EBITDA (in €bn)

The modest growth in EBITDA for the sector on aggregate conceals significant disparities within sub-segments as well as players. The most diversified utilities, both from a business and a geographical perspective, will continue to fare the best, benefiting from continued rapid growth in certain parts of the globe.

In Europe, more than 80% of the electricity sales volumes are hedged a year in advance. As a result, the sector’s cash flow generation from power production and marketing activities in 2026 will largely depend on the prices secured in 2025. With renewables representing a larger share, weather conditions to produce hydro, solar and wind power also play a big role in volumes and remuneration. In 2025, the price for the one-year forward contract for France, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands, Spain and the Nordic market averaged €74/MWh. Italy maintains the highest power price, with the 1Y forward contract averaging €104/MWh in 2025, while the Nordic market pre-sold electricity at €36/MWh. For 2026, pre‑sold electricity volumes will still be 1.3x to 1.4x higher than the pre-Covid era but around 5% lower than a year ago. Further price decreases should be seen in 2027 with forward contracts locked in during 2026 fully reflecting weaker gas prices. Additional capacity in renewables will also keep pressure on prices.

Call 2: Investments expand but at a slower pace

The sector’s investment activity remains robust, but growth is slowing as integrated utilities become more selective in their project pipelines, especially in renewables. The top 40 European utilities will likely invest about €173bn in 2026, a 6% increase compared with 2025.

Investment plans of integrated utilities point to €114bn of spending on infrastructure and projects in 2026, representing 4% growth vs. 2025. Yet disparities exist among European integrated utilities. For instance, EDF SA, Enel SpA (ENLAY), E.ON (ENAKF) and EnBW remain committed to strong capital expenditure increases to build renewable projects and infrastructure, in the case of EDF, nuclear power plants. Other utilities such as EDP, Orsted and Naturgy are expected to see investment levels decline slightly or remain broadly unchanged from 2025 due to a more selective project strategy. The rise of material prices and operating costs has eroded margins and utilities are now more inclined to select the projects offering the best returns.

Capital expenditure is still on the rise, although at a slower pace

Top 40 European utilities' capital expenditure (in €bn)

For pure network utilities, the requirement to expand and modernise grids and gas pipelines should result in an additional 12% increase in capital expenditure in 2026, bringing total investment for the top 20 operators to around €59bn. While German and Dutch Transmission (TSOs) and distribution (DSOs) system operators proportionally have the largest investment programmes, several network utilities have announced new increases. French TSO, Réseau des Transport d’ Electricité will increase investments significantly. Between 2025 and 2040, investment needs are now estimated at €100bn. In Italy, Terna announced an ambitious investment programme to accommodate new renewables and interconnections while Snam will invest in renewable gases and new infrastructure. The same is true for Belgian TSOs. In the Nordics, the push for electrification and on/offshore wind integration obliges players such as Fingrid and Statnett to increase capital expenditure as well.

Inflation and the energy crisis in 2021 and 2022 impacted transmission and distribution network utilities, resulting in higher operating costs and materials. Some European regulators have already adapted their regulatory frameworks to allow TSOs and DSOs to recoup these past costs. The sub-sector’s large investment needs have also driven these changes. In the Netherlands, 2026 is the final year of the current regulatory framework, which has seen a change of methodology to take operating costs into consideration. The Dutch regulatory body, the ACM, is now contemplating a cost-plus methodology for the next regulatory period. Germany is also working on the introduction of a cost-plus system, while it has already existed in Belgium for a long period of time. The methodology is now considered the most appropriate in an environment of long-term investments, as it takes into consideration the variation in inflation, operating costs, funding costs and other capital market elements, ensuring utilities remain financially viable.

Call 3: Spiralling debt means network utilities have to tackle funding challenges

The elevated level of capex, particularly for network utilities, is a source of concern for companies’ credit metrics and funding capacities. Integrated utilities have benefitted from large cash flow generation thanks to their diversified business model. Grid operators, due to their capped revenues, have seen investment needs reaching levels far above their self-financing capacities since 2022. The situation has kept deteriorating, and we expect network utilities’ capital expenditure to represent 164% of their EBITDA on average in 2026.

For network utilities, cash flow generation is not sufficient to cover investment needs

European integrated and network utilities: evolution of the cap-to-EBITDA ratio over the years

In 2023, utilities issued €68bn in bonds on the EUR market. In 2024, the amount reached €72bn. In 2025, EUR bond issuance decreased to €62bn. Several utilities have looked for alternative funding solutions other than bank loans and bonds. Dutch utilities have already received shareholder loans and TenneT has had a total of €23bn in loans available to finance the capital expenditure. Future TenneT Netherlands’ senior bonds will benefit from the Dutch state's guarantee and allow issuance at lower yields. TenneT Germany will receive c.€9bn from its new shareholders. With the deal between RWE (RWNEF)(RWNFF) and Apollo Asset Management, Amprion will benefit from additional funding resources that should reassure credit investors. The Belgian electricity and gas distribution utility Fluvius plans to receive €600m from its shareholders in the first half of 2026.

European regulated network utilities will need to continue looking for external funding to keep credit metrics and ratings at acceptable levels. Access to funds will become a major element in the determination of their credit ratings as signalled by rating agencies’ methodology focus. In 2026, we expect utilities to issue around €70bn in EUR, supported by still-growing investments, high bond refinancing needs and more Reverse Yankees. American utilities have also entered a strong investment growth period with capital expenditure growing 10-15%, and issuing in the EUR currency allows them to benefit from lower rates in the current market conditions.

All in all, the European utilities sector should not surprise in 2026, with continuing trends such as moderate earnings growth. Elevated investment needs, in particular for the expansion of the infrastructure, will push network utilities to find diversified sources of financing.

* top 40 European utilities - integrated utilities (Acea, A2A, EDF, EDP, EnBW, Enel, Engie, E.ON, Fortum, Hera, Iberdrola, Naturgy, Orsted, RWE, Suez, Vattenfall, Verbund, Statkraft, Veolia, Centrica). Network utilities (Alliander, Elia, Enagas, Enexis, Eurogrid, Fingrid, Fluvius, Italgas, National Grid, Nederlandse Gasunie, Redeia, RTE, Snam, Statnett, Stedin, TenneT, Terna, Amprion, Ren, Redexis)

data.title: 2026 Thematic Outlook: Charting The Themes Impacting Markets
data.description:
By Jay Jacobs

From AI, to cryptocurrency, to geopolitical fragmentation and infrastructure, themes are driving markets and changing our daily lives. Our 2026 thematic outlook explores 10 key charts that illustrate what we’ve learned, what has changed, and a look ahead at the potential next-order implications of these powerful mega forces.

Transcript

    Brian Dunlap, CIMA - Big themes are driving markets, and investors are paying attention.

    Over the past decade, U.S. thematic funds have grown more than eleven times. That's as a result of investors increasingly looking to capture the long-term trends that are shaping the global economy.

    Our 2026 Thematic Outlook explores the forces that we believe will matter most in the years ahead and offers potential ways for investors to take action.

    Let's start with AI. It's entering its next growth phase, moving beyond just basic chat prompts into more complex tasks, requiring dramatically more computing power. And that surge in demand is driving investment across data centers, energy and digital infrastructure, with the potential economic benefits now materializing in company earnings beyond just the AI mega scalers.

    Next, defense. As global tensions continue to rise, defense is evolving—with traditional defense shifting towards space, automation, and advanced technology. And then there's infrastructure. From power grids to the energy exports and from transportation to water & agriculture, infrastructure is the global backbone that supports our day-to-day lives and is propelling the AI revolution.

    And finally, tokenization. New digital pathways are emerging, opening the doors to invest in assets in entirely new ways.

    Together, these themes may create compelling growth opportunities for the forward-looking investor.

    Explore our 2026 Outlook to get started.

    Themes continue to drive markets

    Themes are driving markets in unprecedented ways - with U.S. thematic fund AUM growing 11x over the past decade.2 Themes like crypto remain top of mind for investors, with the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) remaining the fastest-growing ETP in history.3

    The iShares Trusts are not investment companies registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940, and therefore are not subject to the same regulatory requirements as mutual funds or ETFs registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940. Investments in these products are speculative and involve a high degree of risk.

    Themes are converging in new and impactful ways across AI, digital processes and digital security or “compute & conflict.” The same can be said for infrastructure with the physical buildout and the energy behind it, as we call “pipes & power.” As we see themes continue to grow in prominence, now may be the time to revisit the role they may play in portfolios and the nuances within themes matter.

    Themes aren’t just continuing to dominate headlines — In our view, they’re reshaping our daily lives. With so many themes to choose from, investors may consider using an active thematic rotation strategy. By dynamically shifting exposure toward the most compelling themes, investors can seek to capture evolving trends. For example, as evidenced in the chart below, we look at consumer behaviors and where consumers are shopping and not shopping when looking to cut-back or “tightening their belts” amidst challenging economic conditions. Blending select discretionary and staple companies, investors can potentially capture the theme of shifting consumer behavior. Investors interested in taking an active approach to thematic investing may consider the iShares U.S. Thematic Rotation Active ETF (THRO).

    Dollar stores

    Dollar stores may benefit as consumers move further down the value chain, prioritizing the lowest-cost options.

    Big-box and budget retailers

    Scale, pricing power, and private-label penetration can allow big-box and budget retailers to capture market share as consumers consolidate spending and prioritize value and essentials.

    E-commerce

    Rising fulfillment and shipping costs and a shift away from discretionary spending may challenge e-commerce profitability and growth as consumers have become more price-sensitive

    Restaurants and hospitality

    As consumers cut discretionary spending, dining out and travel are often among the first deferred, particularly for lower- and middle-income households.

    Home construction

    Higher interest rates and tighter household budgets may reduce affordability and discretionary home-related spending, pressuring demand for materials for new construction projects and large-ticket purchases.

    Off-price retailers

    Off-price retailers have benefited from consumers seeking branded goods at discounted prices, while inventory dislocations across the retail supply chain—including those driven by tariff uncertainty—create attractive buying opportunities that increase customer choice and support margins.

    Converging themes: Compute & conflict

    The continued adoption and advancement of AI has wide-ranging implications and “compute” as we know it may be changing. It is our belief that more complex tasks (not just more users) are driving the next leg of “compute” demand.

    Tokens are like the “fuel” AI uses to read information and produce an answer. Simple chats use a little fuel, but harder jobs, like coding help, generating videos, or AI that can complete multiple steps on its own (i.e., agentic AI) use many times more. And when AI has to interpret messy real-world inputs (think sensor data on a self-driving car) and make many decisions in a sequence, token use can jump yet again. So even if the number of AI users stayed the same, total AI computing demand for computer power can still grow as people use AI for more complex tasks.

    Rising token volumes is one sign that AI workloads are increasing, which can translate into ongoing demand for computing power. For example, the chart below shows roughly 17x growth in weekly token usage since 2024 on OpenRouter, a platform that routes AI requests across many different models.

    AI is transitioning into its next growth phase

    Token intensity rises sharply as AI moves beyond chat into reasoning

    Total AI token usage per week (in trillions)

    As AI evolves, it’s essential to look beyond headline-grabbing AI user counts and towards this depth of usage — as we feel fears of entering “bubble” territory may be overstated.

    What's behind AI's next wave of growth?

    Amid the AI revolution, many fear we may be entering a "bubble" - but the data tells a different story

    Investors looking to access the full AI value chain may consider the iShares Future AI & Tech ETF (ARTY) or take an active approach with the iShares A.I. Innovation and Tech Active ETF (BAI), which seeks to maximize total return and invest in companies across the AI tech stack.

    We believe many investors are looking to diversify within their AI exposure, and their attention could soon shift to areas like defense, where spending has risen amidst shifts from physical systems towards digital capabilities. While AI or “digital” infrastructure has garnered more attention than defense in recent years, we believe investor attention could increase as new defense technologies and risks continue to emerge.

    Beyond AI: defense is evolving and gaining importance

    AI-related construction accounts for < 1% of GDP yet has attracted significantly more focus than defense, even as military spending remains 4x higher*

    Investors interested in the theme of U.S. defense may consider the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA) or take an active global approach to companies across aerospace, defense, and cybersecurity with the iShares Defense Industrials Active ETF (IDEF).

    Converging themes: Pipes & power

    Global opportunities are continuing to emerge across infrastructure from digital or AI-driven infrastructure to water & waste and beyond.

    Cumulative infrastructure investment is expected to reach over $100 trillion by 2040

    In our view, this growth in infrastructure is going to require massive amounts of energy to power it. Sourcing that power presents many opportunities (and challenges) across the globe.

    Projected growth in power generation by type (2025-2027)- CAGR %

    Investors interested in infrastructure may take a global active approach with the iShares Infrastructure Active ETF (BILT), which seeks to maximize long-term total returns by investing in securities of infrastructure-related companies. Investors may also seek a U.S.-focused approach with iShares U.S. Infrastructure ETF (IFRA). Investors specifically interested in targeting U.S. power infrastructure exposure may consider the iShares U.S. Power Infrastructure ETF (POWR).

    What comes next?

    As themes continue to drive markets, it’s worth asking ourselves what may come next. One trend we see emerging is new ways to access markets.

    One such way could be through tokenized assets, or assets that have their ownership rights converted into digital tokens on a blockchain. They are rising in popularity, as evidenced by a rise of stablecoin adoption.4 Stablecoins are one example of tokenization, where the underlying asset is a fiat currency like the U.S. Dollar.

    In our view, as tokenization continues to rise, so will the opportunity to access assets beyond cash and U.S. Treasuries via the blockchain.

    Looking ahead: tokenizing the future of investing

    As we look towards the next era of tokenization, Ethereum may be poised to be a beneficiary of growth

    Tokenized assets by blockchain%

    Conclusion

    The acceleration of themes like AI and infrastructure continues to have wide-ranging implications, especially as new sub-themes emerge and converge, including the depth of AI usage, a changing defense landscape, and ongoing concerns around straining energy infrastructure. We believe it is important for investors to continue exploring ways to invest in the forces that are shaping the world around them, as well as to consider what may be coming next.
